pattern: Fixing one diagonal of a candidate square and using coordinate counts lets you enumerate all valid axis-aligned squares through any query point without exhaustive search.
hints: An axis-aligned square is fully determined by two diagonal corners — fix the query point as one corner and pick any stored point at the same y-distance as a candidate opposite corner.
Once you have two diagonal corners you know both remaining corners; multiply the counts of those two corners to get the number of distinct ways to form the square with that diagonal.
Count duplicates carefully: the problem treats identical added points as distinct, so store a frequency count per coordinate rather than a set.

edge_inputs.description: standard sequence matching the problem illustration
count on an empty structure returns 0
duplicate added points multiply the square count accordingly
zero-area square (all four corners coincide) should not be counted — positive area required
query point that lies on the same row/column as added points but cannot form a square
two distinct valid squares share the query point as a corner

stress.description: randomized add and count queries over a moderate coordinate range
stress.note: small coordinate range (0-50) ensures many duplicate points and valid squares form frequently

solutions.python:
from collections import defaultdict


class DetectSquares:
    def __init__(self):
        self.pt_count = defaultdict(int)  # (x, y) -> frequency
        self.x_to_ys = defaultdict(set)   # x -> set of y values seen

    def add(self, point):
        x, y = point[0], point[1]
        self.pt_count[(x, y)] += 1
        self.x_to_ys[x].add(y)

    def count(self, point):
        qx, qy = point[0], point[1]
        total = 0
        # pick a point on the same column (same x as query) as one far corner
        for y3 in self.x_to_ys.get(qx, set()):
            if y3 == qy:
                continue  # zero-area
            side = abs(y3 - qy)
            # the square can extend left or right
            for dx in [side, -side]:
                x3 = qx + dx
                c1 = self.pt_count[(qx, y3)]
                c2 = self.pt_count[(x3, qy)]
                c3 = self.pt_count[(x3, y3)]
                total += c1 * c2 * c3
        return total

complexity.time: O(n) per count where n is distinct y-values on the query x-column; O(1) per add
